qtype: Number properties, rounding, order of operations, place value
tex:
(a) Write the number six and a half million in figures.
\newline

(b) Write 37 508 correct to the nearest thousand.
\newline

(c) From this list of numbers, write down
\[
6   9   100   28   31   1000   32   36
\]

(i) (d) a factor of 18

(e) a multiple of 12

(f) a square number

(g) a prime number

(h) an irrational number.
\newline

(i) Put one pair of brackets in each statement to make it correct.
\newline

(i) (j) $24 - 4 \times 3 + 2 = 62$

(k) $24 - 4 \times 3 + 2 = 4$

(l) Write $\frac{3}{4}$ as a decimal.
\newline

(m) Work out $\frac{3}{7}$ of 126.
\newline

(n) Write down the value of the reciprocal of 0.5 .
\newline

(o) Without using a calculator, work out $5 \frac{2}{3} - 2 \frac{1}{5}$ .
\newline
\\
You must show all your working and give your answer as a mixed number in its simplest form.
\newline

content.content:
Write the number six and a half million in figures.
\newline

Write 37 508 correct to the nearest thousand.
\newline

From this list of numbers, write down
\[
6 \quad 9 \quad 100 \quad 28 \quad 31 \quad 1000 \quad 32 \quad 36
\]
a factor of 18
a multiple of 12
a square number
a prime number

an irrational number.
\newline

Put one pair of brackets in each statement to make it correct.
\newline

Write $\frac{3}{4}$ as a decimal.
\newline

Work out $\frac{3}{7}$ of 126.
\newline

Write down the value of the reciprocal of 0.5 .
\newline

Without using a calculator, work out $5 \frac{2}{3} - 2 \frac{1}{5}$ .
\newline
\\
You must show all your working and give your answer as a mixed number in its simplest form.
\newline

tex:
(a) The bar chart shows the country in which each of 80 students live.
\newline

(i) (b) How many of these students live in Brazil?

(c) In which country do the largest number of these students live?

(d) How many more of these students live in China than live in Australia?

(e) Find the percentage of these students who live in the USA.
\newline

(f) In Hobart, the temperature at 8 am was $-3^\circ$C and the temperature at 3 pm was $7^\circ$C.
\newline

(i) (g) Find the difference in the temperatures between 8 am and 3 pm.
\newline

(h) The temperature at 10 pm was $12^\circ$C lower than at 3 pm.
\newline

Find the temperature at 10 pm.
\newline

(i) The table shows the favourite language that each of 80 students studies.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|}
\hline
Language & Frequency \\
\hline
French & 12 \\
Spanish & 26 \\
English & 42 \\
\hline
Total & 80 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
Complete the pie chart to show this information.
\newline

content.content: How many of these students live in Brazil?
In which country do the largest number of these students live?
How many more of these students live in China than live in Australia?

Find the percentage of these students who live in the USA.
\newline

In Hobart, the temperature at 8 am was $-3^\circ$C and the temperature at 3 pm was $7^\circ$C.
\newline

Find the difference in the temperatures between 8 am and 3 pm.
\newline

The temperature at 10 pm was $12^\circ$C lower than at 3 pm.
\newline

Find the temperature at 10 pm.
\newline

The table shows the favourite language that each of 80 students studies.
\newline

qtype: Interpreting distance-time graph, time calculations, average speed
tex:
A railway line has three stations, Town, Port and Cove.
\newline
\\
Train A leaves Town for Cove and train B leaves Cove for Town.
\newline
\\
Both trains stop at Port.
\newline

(a) Write down the time that train B leaves Cove.
\newline

(b) Write down how long train A stops at Port.
\newline

(c) How many more minutes does train A take to complete the whole journey than train B?

(d) Write down the time that the two trains pass each other.
\newline

(e) Work out the average speed of train A between Town and Cove in kilometres per hour.
\newline

content.content:
Write down the time that train B leaves Cove.
\newline

Write down how long train A stops at Port.
\newline
How many more minutes does train A take to complete the whole journey than train B?

Write down the time that the two trains pass each other.
\newline

Work out the average speed of train A between Town and Cove in kilometres per hour.
\newline

stem.content:
A railway line has three stations, Town, Port and Cove.
\newline
\\
Train A leaves Town for Cove and train B leaves Cove for Town.
\newline
\\
Both trains stop at Port.
\newline

\begin{center}
\relincludegraphics{Figures/Q05-DistanceTimeGraph.
\newline
png}
\end{center}

qtype: algebraic manipulation, substitution, rearranging formula, solving linear equations from word problems
tex:
(a) Simplify.
\newline
\[
5a + 3b + 2a - 4b
\]

(b) $P = 8x + 3y$

Find the value of $x$ when $P = 21$ and $y = -5$.
\newline

(c) Make $v$ the subject of the formula $S = kv^2$.
\newline

(d) Multiply out and simplify.
\newline
\[
(x - 3)(x + 5)
\]

(e) Nasser has $x$ marbles.
\newline
\\
Selina has 15 more marbles than Nasser.
\newline
\\
Hanif has 3 times as many marbles as Selina.
\newline
\\
In total they have 150 marbles.
\newline

Find the value of $x$.
\newline

content.content:
Simplify.
\newline
\[
5a + 3b + 2a - 4b
\]

$P = 8x + 3y$

Find the value of $x$ when $P = 21$ and $y = -5$.
\newline

Make $v$ the subject of the formula $S = kv^2$.
\newline

Multiply out and simplify.
\newline
\[
(x - 3)(x + 5)
\]

Nasser has $x$ marbles.
\newline
\\
Selina has 15 more marbles than Nasser.
\newline
\\
Hanif has 3 times as many marbles as Selina.
\newline
\\
In total they have 150 marbles.
\newline

Find the value of $x$.
\newline

_rawBody:
\begin{parts}
\item Simplify.
\[
5a + 3b + 2a - 4b
\]

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{2}

\item $P = 8x + 3y$

Find the value of $x$ when $P = 21$ and $y = -5$.

\AnswerLine[$x = $][]
\Marks{2}

\item Make $v$ the subject of the formula $S = kv^2$.

\AnswerLine[$v = $][]
\Marks{2}

\item Multiply out and simplify.
\[
(x - 3)(x + 5)
\]

\AnswerLine[][]
\Marks{2}

\item Nasser has $x$ marbles.\\
Selina has 15 more marbles than Nasser.\\
Hanif has 3 times as many marbles as Selina.\\
In total they have 150 marbles.

Find the value of $x$.

\AnswerLine[$x = $][]
\Marks{2}
\end{parts}

qtype: graphs of linear and reciprocal functions, finding equations, plotting, and finding intersections
tex:
(a) 
Find the equation of line $L$ in the form $y = mx + c$.
\newline

(b) (i) (c) On the grid, draw the line $y = x$.
\newline

(d) Write down the coordinates of the point where the line $y = x$ intersects line $L$.
\newline

(e) (i) (f) Complete the table of values for $y = \frac{8}{x}$.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
$x$ & $-5$ & $-4$ & $-3$ & $-2$ & $-1$ & & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
$y$ & $-1.6$ & & $-2.7$ & & & & & & $2.7$ & & $1.6$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

(g) On the grid, draw the graph of $y = \frac{8}{x}$ for $-5 \le x \le -1$ and $1 \le x \le 5$.
\newline

content.content:
On the grid, draw the line $y = x$.
\newline

Write down the coordinates of the point where the line $y = x$ intersects line $L$.
\newline

Complete the table of values for $y = \frac{8}{x}$.
\newline

qtype: Calculations involving money, fractions, ratios, and set theory
tex:
(a) Pure gold costs \$42 per gram.
\newline

The fraction of pure gold in an object is measured in carats.
\newline
\\
One carat means $\frac{1}{24}$ of the mass of an object is pure gold.
\newline
\\
Henry buys a 9-carat gold bracelet weighing 16 g.
\newline
\\
The price of the bracelet is \$204.
\newline

Is the price of the bracelet more or less than the cost of the pure gold in it?\\
You must show your working.
\newline

(b) A clock made of metals has a mass of 1080 g.
\newline
\\
The mass of each metal in the clock is in the ratio
\[
copper : zinc : other metals = 21 : 14 : 1
\]

Calculate the mass of copper in this clock.
\newline

(c) There are 110 people in a group.
\newline
\\
$G = \{ \text{people who own gold jewellery} \}$\\
$S = \{ \text{people who own silver jewellery} \}$

(d) 18 people own both gold jewellery and silver jewellery.
\newline

(e) 46 people own gold jewellery.
\newline

(f) 11 people own no gold jewellery and no silver jewellery.
\newline

(i) Complete the Venn diagram.
\newline

(ii) Write down $n(G \cup S)$.
\newline

(iii) One of the 110 people is chosen at random.
\newline

Write down the probability that this person owns gold jewellery but not silver jewellery.
\newline

Use set notation to describe the shaded region.
\newline

content.content:
Pure gold costs \$42 per gram.
\newline

The fraction of pure gold in an object is measured in carats.
\newline
\\
One carat means $\frac{1}{24}$ of the mass of an object is pure gold.
\newline
\\
Henry buys a 9-carat gold bracelet weighing 16 g.
\newline
\\
The price of the bracelet is \$204.
\newline

Is the price of the bracelet more or less than the cost of the pure gold in it?\\
You must show your working.
\newline

A clock made of metals has a mass of 1080 g.
\newline
\\
The mass of each metal in the clock is in the ratio
\[
\text{copper} : \text{zinc} : \text{other metals} = 21 : 14 : 1
\]

Calculate the mass of copper in this clock.
\newline

There are 110 people in a group.
\newline
\\
$G = \{ \text{people who own gold jewellery} \}$\\
$S = \{ \text{people who own silver jewellery} \}$

\begin{parts}

18 people own both gold jewellery and silver jewellery.
\newline

46 people own gold jewellery.
\newline

11 people own no gold jewellery and no silver jewellery.
\newline
